channel.title: NPR Topics: Space
channel.description: NPR coverage of space exploration, space shuttle missions, news from NASA, private space exploration, satellite technology, and new discoveries in astronomy and astrophysics.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: In tight quarters, Artemis II astronauts stay fit with the flywheel
item.description: The human body isn't meant to exist without gravity. The astronauts aboard Artemis II are combatting potential physiological damage using an elegantly engineered exercise machine called the flywheel.
item.content:encoded: The human body isn't meant to exist without gravity. The astronauts aboard Artemis II are combatting potential physiological damage using an elegantly engineered exercise machine called the flywheel.
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: Photos and data from Artemis II mission could help determine the moon's origins
item.description: The crew of the Orion spacecraft continue to beam back images from their lunar flyby. The photos reveal previously unseen details of the far side of the moon.
item.content:encoded: The crew of the Orion spacecraft continue to beam back images from their lunar flyby. The photos reveal previously unseen details of the far side of the moon.
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne

item.title: The Artemis II crew saw parts of the moon never seen before. Here's what they said
item.description: The astronauts on Artemis II observed parts of the moon humans had never seen before. Their findings provide a scientific baseline — and sense of wonder — for future missions.
item.content:encoded: The astronauts on Artemis II observed parts of the moon humans had never seen before. Their findings provide a scientific baseline — and sense of wonder — for future missions.
item.dc:creator: Ava Berger

item.title: Astronauts suggest naming a moon crater 'Carroll' after their commander's late wife
item.description: The Artemis II crew, led by Reid Wiseman, was the first to lay eyes on several craters on the far side of the moon. The astronauts want to name one of them after Carroll Wiseman, who died of cancer in 2020.
item.content:encoded:
The Artemis II crew, led by Reid Wiseman, was the first to lay eyes on several craters on the far side of the moon. The astronauts want to name one of them after Carroll Wiseman, who died of cancer in 2020.

(Image credit: NASA via Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: Photos: NASA releases first images from moon flyby
item.description: During the mission's loop around the moon, the crew took geological observations of places of interest on the lunar surface using their own eyes and snapping thousands of photos of the surface.
item.content:encoded: During the mission's loop around the moon, the crew took geological observations of places of interest on the lunar surface using their own eyes and snapping thousands of photos of the surface.
item.dc:creator: NPR Staff

item.title: Artemis II astronauts swung by the moon, broke an Apollo record, and saw an eclipse
item.description: The NASA moon mission completed several key milestones as its crew looped around the lunar body. It's expected to splash down on Earth on Friday.
item.content:encoded: The NASA moon mission completed several key milestones as its crew looped around the lunar body. It's expected to splash down on Earth on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne

item.title: The history of the out-of-this-world playlist NASA uses to wake up mission crews
item.description: It's become a tradition: NASA's ground control plays music to wake up the astronauts on a mission. NASA's chief historian Brian Odom shares the history of the practice.
item.content:encoded: It's become a tradition: NASA's ground control plays music to wake up the astronauts on a mission. NASA's chief historian Brian Odom shares the history of the practice.
item.dc:creator: Hosts

item.title: What scientists hope to learn from Artemis II's moon mission
item.description: NPR's A Martinez talks to Paul Hayne, a planetary scientist at University of Colorado Boulder, about what he and others working on future moon missions hope to learn from Artemis II.
item.content:encoded: NPR's A Martinez talks to Paul Hayne, a planetary scientist at University of Colorado Boulder, about what he and others working on future moon missions hope to learn from Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: A Martínez

item.title: How the crew of Artemis II reacted to seeing the Moon up close
item.description: As the astronauts of Artemis II pass by the lunar surface, they add to the experiences of other missions that flew to the moon.
item.content:encoded: As the astronauts of Artemis II pass by the lunar surface, they add to the experiences of other missions that flew to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: What can Artemis II astronauts see that satellites haven't captured?
item.description: The astronauts on Artemis II will observe parts of the moon rarely seen by human eyes. A NASA planetary scientist said it will offer a vital perspective for lunar research.
item.content:encoded:
The astronauts on Artemis II will observe parts of the moon rarely seen by human eyes. A NASA planetary scientist said it will offer a vital perspective for lunar research.

(Image credit: NASA)
item.dc:creator: Ava Berger
